threats.title: EIP-712 _RENTAL_ORDER_TYPEHASH Uses abi.encode Instead of abi.encodePacked for Type String Concatenation
threats.hypothesis_description: In _deriveRentalTypehashes, the rentalOrderTypeHash is computed as keccak256(abi.encode(rentalOrderTypeString, hookTypeString, itemTypeString)) instead of keccak256(abi.encodePacked(...)). Per EIP-712, the struct type hash must be the keccak256 of concatenated type strings. abi.encode adds ABI padding between dynamic bytes arguments, producing a completely different hash from the EIP-712 specification. This makes the on-chain type hash incompatible with any standard EIP-712 implementation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The rentPayloadTypeHash correctly uses abi.encodePacked in the same function, creating an inconsistency. The wrong type hash means that any off-chain system computing hashes per the EIP-712 spec will derive different values. If the protocol ever needs cross-contract or cross-system hash verification of rental orders, the mismatch becomes a breaking issue.

proof_of_signal.pattern: rentalOrderTypeHash = keccak256(abi.encode(rentalOrderTypeString, hookTypeString, itemTypeString)) uses abi.encode while rentPayloadTypeHash at lines 351-355 correctly uses abi.encodePacked

preconditions: Contract is deployed and operational
Any operation deriving rental order hashes uses this incorrect type hash

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: abi.encode used for type string concatenation instead of abi.encodePacked
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: abi.encode adds 32-byte length + offset padding per dynamic bytes, changing the hash entirely from EIP-712 spec
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Internal consistency masks the issue but prevents standard EIP-712 interoperability

analysis_questions: Does any off-chain component depend on standard EIP-712 rental order hash computation?
Could a future integration break when computing the hash per spec?
Could the wrong type hash enable hash collisions that the correct hash would prevent?
validation_requirements: Compare on-chain type hash with spec-compliant keccak256(abi.encodePacked(...)) computation
Verify whether off-chain signer uses same incorrect encoding

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An off-chain system computes the rental order hash per EIP-712 spec and gets a different result. This breaks signature verification, order matching, or any cross-system hash comparison. If the protocol transitions to off-chain order verification, this mismatch becomes a critical interoperability failure.
prerequisites: Any system computing rental order hashes following EIP-712 spec

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Signature verification failure; potential for hash collisions under non-standard encoding
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users if off-chain systems rely on standard EIP-712

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test compares on-chain type hash with EIP-712 spec-compliant computation

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: N/A — code defect present at deployment
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare hash outputs between on-chain and spec-compliant computation

threats.title: EIP-712 Hook Hash Does Not keccak256 Dynamic bytes extraData Field
threats.hypothesis_description: _deriveHookHash passes hook.extraData directly to abi.encode instead of keccak256(hook.extraData) as required by EIP-712 for dynamic 'bytes' types. This produces non-standard hashes that deviate from EIP-712 specification, affecting all hash derivations that include hooks (metadata hash, rental order hash, rent payload hash).
threats.investigation_rationale: EIP-712 requires dynamic types (bytes, string, arrays) to be hashed with keccak256 before encoding. The raw abi.encode of bytes includes offset/length/data encoding, not the keccak256 hash. This affects hook-dependent hashes used in both order creation (metadata verification) and order stopping (order hash verification).

proof_of_signal.pattern: keccak256(abi.encode(_HOOK_TYPEHASH, hook.target, hook.itemIndex, hook.extraData)) — hook.extraData is bytes and should be keccak256(hook.extraData)

preconditions: Any rental order with hooks uses this incorrect encoding
Off-chain signer must use the same non-standard encoding for signatures to verify

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Dynamic bytes type encoded raw instead of keccak256'd
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Non-standard EIP-712 encoding; if off-chain signer follows spec, signatures will never verify on-chain

analysis_questions: Does the off-chain CREATE_SIGNER use the same non-standard encoding?
Could different extraData values produce hash collisions under raw encoding?
Does this affect the uniqueness guarantees of order hashes?
validation_requirements: Compare _deriveHookHash output with EIP-712 compliant computation
Test signature verification with hooks that have varying extraData

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If the protocol signer is updated to use standard EIP-712 encoding for hook hashes, all hook-dependent operations will fail because on-chain and off-chain hashes diverge. Alternatively, under the current non-standard encoding, different extraData values that happen to produce the same ABI encoding could collide.
prerequisites: Hooks are used in rental orders

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Signature verification failures; potential hash collision attacks
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users creating rentals with hooks

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test validates hook hash against EIP-712 spec

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: N/A — code defect present at deployment
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Hash comparison between on-chain and spec-compliant computation

threats.title: Guard Reverts on Calldata Less Than 4 Bytes Trapping ETH in Rental Safes
threats.hypothesis_description: Guard.checkTransaction reverts if data.length < 4, blocking all transactions from the rental Safe that have empty or short calldata. This prevents the Safe owner from sending ETH via native transfers (which use empty calldata). Any ETH received by the rental Safe (e.g., from game rewards, refunds, or accidental sends) becomes permanently trapped.
threats.investigation_rationale: The requirement for data.length >= 4 at Guard.sol line 292 means the Safe cannot execute a simple ETH transfer. Gnosis Safe's execTransaction with empty data and non-zero value is the standard way to send ETH. This path is blocked, creating permanent ETH lock in rental Safes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (data.length < 4) { revert Errors.GuardPolicy_FunctionSelectorRequired(); }

preconditions: Rental Safe receives ETH through any mechanism (game rewards, direct sends, refunds)
Safe owner attempts to send ETH with empty calldata

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Minimum 4-byte calldata requirement blocks native ETH transfers
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: ETH has no extraction mechanism from rental Safes
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No special handling for value-only transactions with empty data

analysis_questions: Can ETH be sent from the Safe using any other method (e.g., WETH wrap/transfer)?
Is there a whitelisted delegate or module that could extract ETH?
How likely are rental Safes to receive ETH in practice?
validation_requirements: Test ETH send from rental Safe with empty calldata
Verify that no workaround exists for ETH extraction

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: ETH is sent to a rental Safe (e.g., from a game contract reward). The Safe owner cannot send it out because the guard requires at least 4 bytes of calldata. The ETH remains permanently trapped. In gaming use cases, this could amount to significant trapped value.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of ETH sent to rental Safes
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All rental Safe owners who receive ETH

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test covers ETH transfer from rental Safe

threats.title: Pro-Rata Calculation Overflow DoS Prevents Early Stop of PAY Orders
threats.hypothesis_description: In PaymentEscrow._calculatePaymentProRata, the expression `(amount * elapsedTime) * 1000` can overflow for large ERC20 amounts. With Solidity 0.8.x checked arithmetic, this overflow causes a revert, preventing pro-rata settlement and thus blocking early termination of PAY orders. The lender must wait until full expiry to stop the rental.
threats.investigation_rationale: uint256 max is ~1.15e77. For a token with 18 decimals, amount of 1e30 (1 trillion tokens), elapsedTime of 1e7 seconds (~115 days), and multiplied by 1000 gives ~1e40, which is safe. But for amount of 1e59 with any elapsedTime > 1, overflow occurs. While extreme, worthless ERC20 tokens with 18 decimals could be used in PAY orders with astronomical amounts to grief lenders.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 numerator = (amount * elapsedTime) * 1000; — triple multiplication without overflow protection

preconditions: PAY order with very large ERC20 amount (possible with worthless tokens)
Lender attempts to stop the rental before expiry (triggers pro-rata)
amount * elapsedTime * 1000 exceeds uint256 max

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unchecked triple multiplication can overflow for large amounts
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Overflow causes revert in checked Solidity 0.8, blocking pro-rata settlement
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No overflow-safe math or amount bounds check

analysis_questions: What is the minimum ERC20 amount needed to trigger overflow for typical rental durations?
Can the protocol signer prevent large-amount orders?
Does the full-expiry settlement path avoid the pro-rata calculation?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact overflow threshold for various rental durations
Test settlePayment with overflow-triggering amounts
Verify that waiting until expiry allows settlement via the non-pro-rata path

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker creates a PAY order with a worthless ERC20 at maximum uint256 amount. Lender cannot stop the rental early because _calculatePaymentProRata overflows. Lender's NFTs are locked until the rental expires, potentially for a very long duration.
prerequisites: PAY order with very large ERC20 amount

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Lender's NFTs locked for full rental duration; opportunity cost
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Lenders in PAY orders with large-amount tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Assets recoverable after rental expiry via non-pro-rata path

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test covers large-amount pro-rata calculation

threats.title: Kernel can be permanently bricked by setting executor/admin to address(0) via executeAction (no zero-address guard)
threats.hypothesis_description: Kernel.executeAction allows Actions.ChangeExecutor and Actions.ChangeAdmin without validating target_ != address(0). If set to zero, no one can execute privileged lifecycle actions or manage roles, effectively freezing governance and potentially preventing module upgrades/fixes.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code defect with immediate protocol-level DoS impact if accidentally invoked; it’s also a relevant attack in the case of compromised executor/admin wanting to permanently sabotage recovery.

proof_of_signal.lines: executeAction: ChangeExecutor/ChangeAdmin branches set executor/admin = target_ without checks
proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing non-zero validation on critical governance addresses

preconditions: Caller is current Kernel.executor (for executeAction)
Action is ChangeExecutor or ChangeAdmin with target_=address(0)

analysis_questions: Is there any recovery path if executor/admin becomes zero (e.g., through migration)?
Do deployment scripts or operational runbooks prevent this at tooling level?

Assess whether any module/policy can still function safely without governance

threats.title: Create2Deployer.generateSaltWithSender assembly uses shr on bytes12 data, potentially collapsing entropy and causing salt collisions across different data inputs
threats.hypothesis_description: generateSaltWithSender combines sender and data with `salt := or(shl(0x60, sender), shr(0xA0, data))`. Right-shifting a bytes12 by 160 bits likely zeros it (or misplaces bits), meaning `data` may contribute little/no entropy. This can lead to accidental salt collisions and unexpected Create2 address collisions for a given sender.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code-level correctness issue in salt derivation. If the protocol relies on (sender,data) uniqueness to derive distinct addresses across deployments/versions, collisions can break deployment guarantees and potentially DoS future deployments at expected addresses.

proof_of_signal.lines: generateSaltWithSender: salt := or(shl(0x60, sender), shr(0xA0, data))
proof_of_signal.pattern: Suspicious bit shifting: shifting a 12-byte value right by 20 bytes risks zeroing it, reducing uniqueness

preconditions: Protocol/users rely on generateSaltWithSender to avoid collisions across multiple deployments per sender
Two deployments use different data values expecting distinct salts

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: bitwise packing likely incorrect for bytes12 component
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: salt collisions -> inability to deploy at intended deterministic addresses
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No explicit tests/assertions that data affects salt; no alternative hash-based salt derivation

analysis_questions: Does shr(0xA0, data) always become 0 for bytes12? If so, salt depends only on sender.
Is generateSaltWithSender actually used in deployment scripts (BaseDeploy._generateSaltWithSender)?
Can collisions lead to protocol misconfiguration or inability to redeploy upgraded components at expected addresses?
validation_requirements: Unit test: compare salts for same sender with different data; verify if identical
If identical, compute Create2 addresses and show collision/DoS behavior

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Operator attempts to deploy v2 contracts with different protocolVersion (data) but gets same salt, causing deployments to collide at same Create2 addresses and revert/DoS, blocking upgrades or multi-environment deployments.
prerequisites: Use of generateSaltWithSender for multiple deployments per sender

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Operational/upgrade DoS; could delay patches and deployments
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol operators; downstream users if upgrades blocked
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: High; can change salt derivation (hash-based) and redeploy with corrected tooling

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing deterministic salt uniqueness tests for generateSaltWithSender

threats.title: Storage.contractToHook returns zero if hookStatus[hook]==0, but Guard.checkTransaction separately checks hookOnTransaction(hook) using hook address; inconsistent hook enablement logic could lead to unexpected bypass/DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: Storage.contractToHook returns address(0) when hookStatus[hook]==0, but Guard.checkTransaction then calls STORE.hookOnTransaction(hook) on the returned hook. Since contractToHook already filters by hookStatus!=0, hookOnTransaction becomes redundant, and any misconfiguration of hookStatus bits vs path can unexpectedly switch between hook-mediated enforcement and default _checkTransaction behavior. This can either unintentionally weaken enforcement (if hook should be used but is filtered out) or unexpectedly route to hook (if path points to enabled hook) and bypass default selector checks.
threats.investigation_rationale: Hook routing is a critical trust boundary; subtle inconsistencies can create real-world security incidents due to configuration mistakes. Because hooks fully replace _checkTransaction checks when active, ensuring consistent, explicit enablement is important.
deployment_assumptions: GUARD_ADMIN configures hook paths/statuses over time
At least one hook is used in production for transaction middleware
threats.config_evidence: Storage.updateHookPath and Storage.updateHookStatus exist; Guard.updateHookPath/updateHookStatus are role-gated but no timelock; no deployment script shows initial hook configuration.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Dual hook enablement checks split across contractToHook() and hookOnTransaction(), increasing misconfiguration risk in a security-critical bypass path

preconditions: A hook path exists for some `to` address
hookStatus bits are toggled in ways that do not match operator intent

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: Storage.sol contractToHook + Guard.sol checkTransaction hook routing
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: complex enablement logic on critical bypass path
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: operator mistakes can silently enable hook bypass or disable intended checks
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No events in Storage for hook path/status changes shown here (verify Events library); no guard fallback checks when hook active

analysis_questions: Is hookStatus intended to act as a global on/off, or per-function bitmap? (it is a bitmap; contractToHook treats any nonzero as enabled)
Can a hook be enabled for onStart/onStop only (bitmap 2/4) but still be returned by contractToHook, causing Guard to query hookOnTransaction and skip forwarding (fall back to _checkTransaction)?
Do operators expect hook routing to exist even when onTransaction bit is off?
validation_requirements: Simulate: hookStatus=2 (onStart only), path set; confirm contractToHook returns hook (nonzero) but Guard sees hookOnTransaction false and uses _checkTransaction
Simulate: hookStatus=1 and path set; confirm full bypass to hook

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: GUARD_ADMIN enables a hook for lifecycle hooks (onStart/onStop) but accidentally sets bitmap such that contractToHook returns the hook for Guard routing decisions; depending on bits, Guard may unexpectedly route or not route, potentially allowing a buggy hook to become active onTransaction or disabling intended middleware checks, leading to asset transfer bypass if hook fails open.
prerequisites: Human/configuration error or buggy hook contract

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Misconfiguration can cause guard bypass (NFT theft) or DoS (transactions reverted unexpectedly)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Rental safes interacting with hooked targets
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: High; configuration fix if detected early

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests around hookStatus bitmap combinations and Guard routing behavior

threats.title: rentalWallet Omitted from _deriveRentalOrderHash Enables Order Hash Collision and State Corruption
threats.hypothesis_description: The _deriveRentalOrderHash function excludes the rentalWallet field from the EIP-712 encoding despite the type string declaring 'address rentalWallet'. This means RentalOrder structs differing only in rentalWallet produce identical hashes. An attacker calling stopRent can substitute a different rentalWallet in the order struct and still pass the hash existence check in Storage.removeRentals. This causes assets to be reclaimed from the wrong Safe, rentalAsset counters for the wrong wallet to be decremented, and the original wallet's rental counters to remain permanently elevated, locking assets behind the guard forever.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect: the abi.encode call in _deriveRentalOrderHash goes from order.renter directly to order.startTimestamp, skipping order.rentalWallet. The hash output is identical regardless of rentalWallet value, allowing callers to supply arbitrary wallet addresses to stopRent while matching stored order hashes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.encode skips order.rentalWallet: encodes order.renter then order.startTimestamp directly, missing the rentalWallet field between them

preconditions: An active rental order exists in Storage.orders
Attacker knows all fields of the active rental order (emitted in RentalOrderStarted event)
A substitute rental safe (protocol-deployed) holds matching ERC721/ERC1155 tokens for the reclaim to succeed, OR for ERC1155 the rentedAssets count at the substitute wallet must not underflow

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: abi.encode(_RENTAL_ORDER_TYPEHASH, order.seaportOrderHash, ..., order.renter, order.startTimestamp, order.endTimestamp) — rentalWallet absent
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Caller of stopRent provides fabricated rentalWallet that matches a different Safe, while the computed hash still equals the stored order hash
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can force reclaim from wrong safe (stealing ERC721/ERC1155 if present), permanently lock original safe's assets (rentedAssets never decremented), and potentially corrupt escrow settlement

threats.title: orderType and emittedExtraData Omitted from _deriveOrderMetadataHash Allowing Fulfiller to Alter Order Semantics
threats.hypothesis_description: The _deriveOrderMetadataHash function only encodes rentDuration and hooks into the hash, omitting orderType and emittedExtraData despite both being declared in the OrderMetadata EIP-712 type string. This means the lender's Seaport zone hash does not cryptographically bind the order type. A fulfiller can substitute orderType without invalidating the zone hash check in _isValidOrderMetadata. While current item-type validation in _convertToItems mitigates cross-type conversion for most cases, the missing binding violates the security model and any future code path that relaxes item constraints would enable payment flow reversal.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code clearly shows only two fields encoded: _ORDER_METADATA_TYPEHASH, metadata.rentDuration, keccak256(abi.encodePacked(hookHashes)). The type string declares 4 fields: orderType, rentDuration, hooks, emittedExtraData. This is a demonstrable encoding mismatch.

proof_of_signal.pattern: abi.encode(_ORDER_METADATA_TYPEHASH, metadata.rentDuration, keccak256(hookHashes)) — metadata.orderType and keccak256(metadata.emittedExtraData) are absent

preconditions: Fulfiller receives a valid server-side signature and payload for an order
Fulfiller modifies metadata.orderType before calling Seaport fulfill

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Only 2 of 4 declared fields encoded in metadata hash
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Fulfiller changes orderType from BASE to PAY or vice versa without invalidating zoneHash
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Currently mitigated by item-type validation but emittedExtraData injection is immediately exploitable for event log manipulation; orderType swap becomes exploitable with any future relaxation of item constraints

threats.title: Guard Completely Bypassed When Hook Is Active — Rented Assets Transferable
threats.hypothesis_description: In Guard.checkTransaction, when a hook is registered and active for a target contract address, execution flow is forwarded entirely to the hook via _forwardToHook and the _checkTransaction function is NOT called. This means all standard protections (blocking NFT transfers, approvals, module changes, guard removal) are completely delegated to the hook contract. If a hook has any bug, missing check, or doesn't replicate all guard protections, rented assets can be transferred out of the safe. The guard provides ZERO fallback enforcement when a hook is active.
threats.investigation_rationale: The if/else branch at lines where hook != address(0) && isActive clearly shows _forwardToHook is called and _checkTransaction is in the else branch, meaning they are mutually exclusive. A single buggy hook compromises all guard protections for that target.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (hook != address(0) && isActive) { _forwardToHook(...); } else { _checkTransaction(...); } — mutually exclusive branches, hook bypasses all standard checks

preconditions: GUARD_ADMIN has set a hook for a target contract address
The hook contract does not replicate all Guard._checkTransaction protections
Renter initiates a transaction from the rental safe to the hooked target

threats.title: Guard Bypass via Non-Standard NFT Function Selectors Not Covered by _checkTransaction
threats.hypothesis_description: Guard._checkTransaction only blocks a hardcoded set of ERC721/ERC1155 function selectors (transferFrom, safeTransferFrom, approve, setApprovalForAll, safeBatchTransferFrom). Any non-standard transfer or approval function (e.g., EIP-4494 permit, custom burn, permit-and-transfer patterns) is NOT blocked. If a rented NFT implements additional transfer mechanisms beyond the standard selectors, the renter can use these to move or approve rented assets out of the safe. The guard's else branch for unknown selectors does nothing except check the three specific known-bad selectors.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _checkTransaction function uses an if/else if chain for specific selectors. Any selector not in this chain falls through to the final else block which only checks three hardcoded selectors. All other selectors are implicitly ALLOWED.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Hardcoded selector checks only cover 5 ERC721 and 2 ERC1155 selectors plus 3 Safe selectors. All other selectors pass through without blocking.

preconditions: Rented NFT implements a transfer or approval mechanism with a function selector not in the guard's hardcoded list
Active rental exists with such an NFT in the safe
No hook is active for the NFT contract (or hook doesn't block the selector either)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Guard only checks ~10 selectors; ERC721/ERC1155 contracts can implement arbitrary additional functions
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Renter calls permit(spender, tokenId, deadline, sig) or custom transferNFT() on the rented asset; guard does not block
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Full theft of rented NFT via non-standard transfer/approval mechanism

threats.title: Whitelisted Delegate Call Target Can Bypass Guard and Steal All Rented Assets via Batched Internal Calls
threats.hypothesis_description: If a contract like Gnosis MultiSend is whitelisted as a delegate call target (via toggleWhitelistDelegate), a rental safe owner can delegatecall to it and execute arbitrary internal calls that bypass the guard entirely. When a safe delegates to MultiSend, the internal calls execute in the safe's context but do NOT pass through the safe's execTransaction flow, meaning the guard's checkTransaction is never invoked for those internal calls. The renter can batch transferFrom calls for all rented NFTs within a single MultiSend delegatecall.
threats.investigation_rationale: Guard.checkTransaction allows delegatecalls to whitelisted targets. Delegatecall to a batching contract like MultiSend allows arbitrary internal calls that bypass the guard. Additionally, checkAfterExecution is empty, providing no post-execution verification that assets are still in the safe or that guard/module configuration is preserved.
deployment_assumptions: A batching/multicall contract (e.g., MultiSend) is whitelisted via toggleWhitelistDelegate
threats.config_evidence: Deployment script DeployProtocol.s.sol does not show specific whitelist entries but the toggleWhitelistDelegate function exists and is callable by ADMIN_ADMIN role.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (operation == Enum.Operation.DelegateCall && !STORE.whitelistedDelegates(to)) revert — whitelisted delegates pass without further inspection of delegated logic

threats.title: CEI Violation in stopRent: Reentrancy via NFT Callback Before Storage Removal Enables Cross-Order Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: In stopRent, _reclaimRentedItems transfers NFTs back to the lender via safeTransferFrom, which triggers onERC721Received/onERC1155Received callbacks on the lender. At this point, the order is still active in Storage (orders[hash] == true) and rentalAssets haven't been decremented. A malicious lender contract can re-enter stopRent or stopRentBatch for a DIFFERENT order during this callback, potentially draining escrow funds or corrupting rental asset tracking. In stopRentBatch, the reentrancy window is even wider since all reclaims happen before any settlements or storage removals.
threats.investigation_rationale: The execution order in stopRent is: _removeHooks → _reclaimRentedItems → ESCRW.settlePayment → STORE.removeRentals. External calls with callbacks occur at steps 1 and 2, before any state cleanup at steps 3 and 4. This violates CEI pattern.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _reclaimRentedItems (external NFT transfer with callbacks) at line 226 before STORE.removeRentals at line 237; order still in storage during callback

preconditions: Lender is a smart contract implementing onERC721Received/onERC1155Received
Lender has multiple active rental orders
Lender's callback function re-enters stopRent for a different order

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Lender receives NFT callback → re-enters stopRent for order B → order B fully settled and removed → original stopRent continues with potentially altered escrow state
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Double-settlement of same tokens if escrow state is corrupted, or DoS of batch operations

threats.title: PAYEE Orders Have Zero On-Chain Footprint — Assets Unrecoverable by Protocol
threats.hypothesis_description: In Create._rentFromZone, when the order type is PAYEE, the entire storage recording block is skipped. No order hash is stored in Storage.orders, no rental asset updates are recorded, no ERC20 deposit increments occur, and no hooks are processed. PAYEE orders exist only as Seaport order events. If the paired PAY order fails, is never created, or is stopped independently, the PAYEE-side assets (NFTs and ERC20s transferred via Seaport considerations) have no protocol-level tracking and cannot be stopped/reclaimed through the protocol's mechanisms.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _rentFromZone function has an explicit condition: if (payload.metadata.orderType.isBaseOrder() || payload.metadata.orderType.isPayOrder()). PAYEE orders fall into neither branch, so all storage, escrow, and hook logic is bypassed. The validateOrder function still returns success, validating the Seaport order.

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Assets delivered via PAYEE fulfillment are effectively unrecoverable if PAY counterpart fails

threats.title: Immutable Domain Separator Enables Cross-Chain Signature Replay After Chain Fork
threats.hypothesis_description: The EIP-712 domain separator is computed at deployment as an immutable value using the deployment-time block.chainid. While _CHAIN_ID is stored, it is never compared against block.chainid at runtime in _recoverSignerFromPayload. After a chain fork (e.g., ETH/ETC-style split), the domain separator remains valid on both chains, enabling replay of rental creation signatures. An attacker can take a valid signature from one chain and replay it on the forked chain to create unwanted rentals.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Signer constructor stores _CHAIN_ID but _recoverSignerFromPayload always uses _DOMAIN_SEPARATOR (immutable) without checking if block.chainid still matches _CHAIN_ID. This violates EIP-712's recommendation to recompute domain separator when chain ID changes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _DOMAIN_SEPARATOR.toTypedDataHash(payloadHash) always uses immutable separator; _CHAIN_ID stored but never checked against block.chainid

preconditions: A chain fork occurs creating two chains with different chain IDs
Protocol is deployed on both chains (or the forked chain has the same deployment)
Valid signatures exist from before the fork

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Inflate Escrow Tracked Balance Causing Settlement Failures
threats.hypothesis_description: PaymentEscrow.increaseDeposit records the nominal amount from the order, but fee-on-transfer ERC20 tokens deliver less than the specified amount. This inflates balanceOf[token] relative to the actual token balance. When settlePayment attempts to transfer the full pre-fee amount, it may fail due to insufficient actual balance, permanently locking lender's NFTs because stopRent cannot complete.

After multiple orders, the tracked balance exceeds the real balance. During settlement, _safeTransfer attempts to transfer amounts that don't exist, reverting the entire stopRent transaction.

threats.title: Stop policy accepts arbitrary RentalOrder data (no on-chain existence check) enabling asset theft via early reclaim + forced revert
threats.hypothesis_description: Stop.stopRent() and stopRentBatch() accept a full RentalOrder struct from an untrusted caller but do not verify that the order exists in Storage before performing external interactions. The only existence check is implicitly deferred to STORE.removeRentals(), which is called after hooks, safe delegatecall reclaim, and escrow settlement. An attacker can craft an order referencing a protocol-deployed Safe they own (or any Safe with Stop module enabled), include any NFTs currently held by that Safe as 'rental items', and force the Safe to delegatecall reclaimRentalOrder(), which will transfer those NFTs to the attacker-chosen 'lender'. The attacker can then intentionally make STORE.removeRentals() revert (e.g., by using a non-existent orderHash), but because the Safe execTransactionFromModule call is a separate internal transaction whose effects are not reverted by Stop's later revert if the Safe call succeeds, this could result in permanent NFT loss from the Safe (requires validation of Safe module call atomicity and whether Stop reverts after Safe call).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete unprivileged on-chain path: stopRent is public and performs a delegatecall-based asset transfer from a Safe before confirming an active order. If the Safe module execution is not fully rolled back by the Stop revert (or if the Safe call returns success even when internal token transfers succeed), this becomes direct theft. Even if atomicity holds, the pattern is still an operationally critical DoS/griefing vector because it executes expensive external calls before an existence check.
deployment_assumptions: Rental Safes have Stop policy enabled as a Safe module (Factory.initializeRentalSafe).
Gnosis Safe module execution side effects may persist even if the caller (Stop) later reverts (must be verified against Safe implementation and call semantics).
threats.config_evidence: Factory.sol indicates Stop module is enabled on deployment via initializeRentalSafe(); no explicit pre-check exists in Stop.stopRent prior to reclaim/settlement.

proof_of_signal.lines: stopRent(): external calls to _removeHooks/_reclaimRentedItems/ESCRW.settlePayment occur before STORE.removeRentals
proof_of_signal.pattern: No `STORE.orders[hash]` check before delegatecall reclaim; existence check deferred to `STORE.removeRentals(_deriveRentalOrderHash(order), ...)`

preconditions: Attacker can call Stop.stopRent/stopRentBatch (public).
Target Safe can execute Stop module (module enabled) and holds NFTs the attacker wants to move.
Attacker can craft RentalOrder such that Safe delegatecall to reclaimRentalOrder transfers those NFTs to attacker-controlled lender address.
There exists a way for Stop.stopRent to revert after the Safe call while not reverting the Safe side effects (must be validated).

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Externalized state transition: reclaim and payments happen before verifying Storage.orders[orderHash] exists
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a Safe has Stop module enabled and holds transferable NFTs

operational_questions: Does Safe.execTransactionFromModule() side effects revert if Stop reverts later in the same outer call?

threats.title: Rental Safe deployment can be spoof-registered in Storage, enabling bypass of 'protocol-deployed safe' checks
threats.hypothesis_description: Storage.addRentalSafe(safe) records any address as a protocol-deployed safe without verifying that it is a Safe proxy or has expected module/guard configuration. Factory.deployRentalSafe registers the created safe, but nothing in Storage enforces that only Factory can register, only that the caller is a permissioned policy. If any other activated policy (now or in future) is granted permission to call STORE.addRentalSafe, it could register attacker-controlled contracts as 'deployedSafes', allowing them to pass Create._isValidSafeOwner's STORE.deployedSafes(safe) != 0 gate. This could let attackers rent into non-standard wallets that do not enforce guard/stop invariants, risking NFT theft or inability to reclaim.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a privilege-boundary fragility: the invariant 'only protocol safes can rent' relies on correct permissions and correct safe verification. Operationally, future policy additions or misconfigured permissions can silently break the trust boundary.
deployment_assumptions: Only Factory policy is permissioned for STORE.addRentalSafe in Kernel.modulePermissions.
No other policy can call STORE.addRentalSafe now or after upgrades.
threats.config_evidence: Factory.requestPermissions() requests STORE.addRentalSafe.selector; Storage.addRentalSafe lacks internal validation of Safe code/config.

proof_of_signal.lines: addRentalSafe(): deployedSafes[safe] = newSafeCount; no contract/type checks
proof_of_signal.pattern: No ensureContract(safe), no Safe interface checks, no guard/module verification

preconditions: An attacker (or compromised/buggy new policy) obtains Kernel permission to call STORE.addRentalSafe, or Admin/executor installs a policy that has it.
Attacker registers a non-Safe or modified Safe that doesn't enforce Guard/Stop.
Create.validateOrder later accepts this address as a valid rental safe based solely on deployedSafes mapping.

threats.title: Unrestricted Safe self-call can disable Guard/Stop module and drain rented NFTs via Factory.initializeRentalSafe()
threats.hypothesis_description: Factory.initializeRentalSafe() is externally callable and executes ISafe(address(this)).enableModule() and setGuard() against the calling context. Any rental Safe owner can make the Safe call Factory.initializeRentalSafe(...) (via Safe.execTransaction), causing those privileged Safe configuration changes to be executed by the Safe itself. The Guard policy attempts to block setGuard and restrict module enable/disable, but it only inspects a narrow set of selectors and does not block generic Safe.execTransaction calls; and even if it did, allowing any path that reaches initializeRentalSafe lets the renter set guard to address(0) and/or enable a malicious module. Once the guard is removed or a malicious module is enabled, the renter can transfer rented ERC721/ERC1155 out of the Safe, defeating the protocol’s core custody invariant.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a high-realism, single-transaction on-chain attack that targets the exact Safe hardening workflow. The code comment in Factory explicitly assumes delegatecall is restricted, but the function itself is not restricted to deployment-only execution and can be invoked post-deployment by the Safe.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function initializeRentalSafe(address _stopPolicy, address _guardPolicy) external { ISafe(address(this)).enableModule(_stopPolicy); ISafe(address(this)).setGuard(_guardPolicy); } // no access control, intended for Safe.setup delegatecall

preconditions: Attacker controls at least the Safe threshold for a protocol-deployed rental Safe (i.e., the renter can execute Safe transactions)
Rental Safe currently holds actively rented ERC721/ERC1155
Safe can call external contracts (Factory) via execTransaction (standard Safe behavior)
Guard does not comprehensively block Safe configuration-changing paths (e.g., via execTransaction to call Factory.initializeRentalSafe)

threats.title: Guard bypass via unblocked Safe configuration selectors (execTransaction/manager functions) enables module/guard changes and NFT theft
threats.hypothesis_description: Guard._checkTransaction blocks only a handful of ERC721/ERC1155 selectors and a few Safe selectors (setGuard/enableModule/disableModule) when they appear as the top-level selector in the transaction calldata. Many Safe configuration changes can be executed through other Safe entrypoints (notably Safe.execTransaction) or manager methods that are not enumerated in Guard._checkTransaction. If the Safe owners can invoke a path that changes guard/modules without triggering those exact selectors at the top-level, they can remove Guard or enable a malicious module and then transfer rented NFTs out.
threats.investigation_rationale: Phase A already flags selector-based guard incompleteness. This hypothesis focuses on the most realistic exploit path: using Safe’s standard transaction execution entrypoint(s) that are likely not blocked, rather than exotic token methods.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Selector allowlist/denylist only checks specific selectors; default path allows unknown selectors; no explicit handling for Safe.execTransaction or other Safe management entrypoints

preconditions: Attacker controls a rental Safe’s signing threshold
There exists at least one Safe-callable function path that mutates guard/modules without presenting blocked selectors as the top-level selector to Guard
Active rental asset is in the Safe (rentedAssets positive)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Guard relies on matching raw selectors and fixed offsets; Safe has multiple management-related functions and meta-transaction patterns

operational_questions: Which exact Safe selectors are blocked by RentalConstants, and are all Safe configuration-changing selectors covered?

Does Guard run for module-originated transactions (execTransactionFromModule) differently than owner txs?

Craft PoC Safe tx that changes guard/modules without triggering blocked selectors
Confirm that after change, rented NFT can be transferred

If the GUARD_ADMIN whitelists a hook that does not strictly reimplement the Guard's selector checks, the renter can call the target contract and transfer rented NFTs out of the Safe.
threats.investigation_rationale: Bypassing core security checks in favor of external middleware creates a massive single point of failure if the middleware is flawed.

threats.title: Immutable Domain Separator Enables Cross-Chain Signature Replay After Chain Forks
threats.hypothesis_description: The Signer contract computes _DOMAIN_SEPARATOR once at deployment using block.chainid and stores it as immutable. _CHAIN_ID is stored but never checked at runtime in _recoverSignerFromPayload. After a chain fork where block.chainid changes on one fork, valid rental creation signatures become replayable on both chains because the same domain separator is used, despite the chain IDs diverging.
threats.investigation_rationale: Standard EIP-712 implementations (e.g., OpenZeppelin) recompute the domain separator if block.chainid differs from the stored chain ID. The Signer contract stores _CHAIN_ID but never compares it to block.chainid before using _DOMAIN_SEPARATOR. After a fork, signatures intended for one chain work on both.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _recoverSignerFromPayload uses _DOMAIN_SEPARATOR directly without checking block.chainid == _CHAIN_ID

preconditions: A chain fork occurs where block.chainid changes on the forked chain
Valid protocol signer signatures exist from before the fork
Both chains have the same protocol deployment

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Same signature validates on both chains after fork
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Post-fork, for all existing valid signatures

operational_questions: Is a chain fork realistically expected for the target chains?
Could signature expiration mitigate replay (how long are signatures valid)?
Would replayed rentals on the forked chain cause actual harm?
validation_requirements: Verify _CHAIN_ID is never compared to block.chainid in any code path
Test signature validity after simulated chain fork
Assess if existing signature expiration limits the replay window

threats.title: Kernel can be irreversibly bricked by setting executor/admin to address(0), permanently halting security operations
threats.hypothesis_description: Kernel.executeAction() supports Actions.ChangeExecutor and Actions.ChangeAdmin but does not validate target_ != address(0). If executor is set to zero, no one can call executeAction again, preventing module upgrades, policy activation/deactivation, or kernel migration. If admin is set to zero, no one can grant/revoke roles, preventing operational response (e.g., revoking CREATE_SIGNER) and policy admin actions that rely on roles.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an operational footgun that could be triggered accidentally or by a compromised executor. It directly blocks incident response and upgrade paths.
deployment_assumptions: Executor/admin are EOAs/multisigs that can make mistakes or be compromised.
threats.config_evidence: Kernel.sol executeAction() assigns executor/admin directly with no zero-address guard.

proof_of_signal.lines: executeAction(): else if action_ == ChangeExecutor { executor = target_; } else if ChangeAdmin { admin = target_; }
proof_of_signal.pattern: No require(target_ != address(0))

preconditions: Caller is current Kernel.executor.
A ChangeExecutor/ChangeAdmin action is executed with target_ = address(0).

initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time executor can call executeAction

threats.title: Factory.initializeRentalSafe is externally callable and can be abused as a generic Safe reconfiguration primitive
threats.hypothesis_description: Factory.initializeRentalSafe(_stopPolicy,_guardPolicy) is external and performs ISafe(address(this)).enableModule and setGuard on the calling context. While intended to be used only via Safe.setup delegatecall during deployment, any Safe can choose to delegatecall into Factory.initializeRentalSafe at any time. If a user-controlled Safe delegatecalls it, the Safe can enable arbitrary module and set arbitrary guard by passing attacker-chosen addresses, potentially creating confusion around what is a 'protocol-configured' safe and, if such a Safe is later registered (see addRentalSafe risk), it could bypass protocol assumptions.
threats.investigation_rationale: Not directly exploitable without additional misconfig, but it is a sharp edge: a public helper that mutates Safe configuration based on untrusted parameters. This is worth validating against operational assumptions and to ensure no path exists for rental safes to reach it despite guard restrictions.
deployment_assumptions: Rental safe owners cannot delegatecall Factory.initializeRentalSafe due to Guard delegatecall whitelist restrictions.
Factory contract is not whitelisted as a delegatecall target in Storage.whitelistedDelegates.
threats.config_evidence: Factory.sol exposes initializeRentalSafe externally with no access control or context check.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External function with privileged Safe mutations, no caller/context validation

preconditions: A Safe can execute a delegatecall to Factory.initializeRentalSafe (either by being a non-rental safe, or due to misconfigured whitelistedDelegates allowing a path to delegatecall Factory).
Caller supplies arbitrary _stopPolicy/_guardPolicy.

threats.title: Storage.updateHookPath can create a silent guard bypass if hook is enabled but does not enforce baseline selector blocks
threats.hypothesis_description: Guard.checkTransaction forwards to hook.onTransaction(safe,to,value,data) when a hook is configured for a target and hookOnTransaction is enabled, and then skips _checkTransaction entirely. GUARD_ADMIN can map any target contract to any hook via Guard.updateHookPath(), and enable onTransaction via updateHookStatus(). If a hook implementation is permissive/buggy, safe owners can execute previously blocked operations (e.g., setGuard, enableModule, NFT transfers) by targeting that contract.

deployment_assumptions: GUARD_ADMIN may configure hooks for production targets.
Hook contracts are not formally verified to enforce the baseline guard rules.

threats.title: Whitelisted Delegate Can Overwrite Guard Storage Slot to Disable All Rental Protections
threats.hypothesis_description: Guard.checkTransaction allows delegatecall to whitelisted delegate targets. In delegatecall context, the called contract executes with the Safe's storage. A whitelisted delegate could overwrite the Safe's guard storage slot (Gnosis Safe internal slot) to address(0), permanently removing the guard. checkAfterExecution is empty and performs no post-execution integrity checks. Once the guard is removed, the renter can freely transfer all rented NFTs out of the Safe.
threats.investigation_rationale: Delegatecall gives the callee full storage access. The guard only checks pre-execution (checkTransaction); checkAfterExecution is a no-op. No post-execution verification of guard/module integrity exists.
deployment_assumptions: A whitelisted delegate has the ability (intentionally or via vulnerability) to write to arbitrary storage slots

threats.title: Kernel ChangeExecutor/ChangeAdmin to address(0) Permanently Bricks Protocol Governance
threats.hypothesis_description: Kernel.executeAction with Actions.ChangeExecutor or Actions.ChangeAdmin directly sets executor or admin to the target address without validating that target != address(0). If executor is set to address(0), no further module installations, policy activations, or kernel migrations can occur. If admin is set to address(0), no roles can be granted or revoked. This permanently bricks all protocol governance.
threats.investigation_rationale: Unlike InstallModule and ActivatePolicy which call ensureContract(target_), ChangeExecutor and ChangeAdmin have no validation on the target address. A single accidental or malicious transaction irreversibly destroys protocol governance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ChangeExecutor: executor = target_ with no address(0) check; ChangeAdmin: admin = target_ with no address(0) check

threats.title: Kernel policy deactivation can leave stale module permissions due to dynamic requestPermissions() symmetry failure
threats.hypothesis_description: Kernel._deactivatePolicy calls policy.requestPermissions() at deactivation time to decide what to revoke. If a policy’s requestPermissions output can differ over time (e.g., due to upgrade/migration patterns, selector changes in modules, or a malicious policy), some permissions granted at activation could remain enabled after deactivation. This creates a latent privilege surface where an inactive policy can still call permissioned module functions (including upgrades/freeze/whitelist toggles), enabling unexpected protocol control paths.
threats.investigation_rationale: While exploitation likely requires executor/admin-level involvement (LOW realism), the resulting operational failure mode is severe: permission matrix drift that is hard to reason about and may enable unauthorized module calls by supposedly inactive policies.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _deactivatePolicy fetches Permissions[] again from policy_.requestPermissions() instead of storing granted set at activation

preconditions: A policy’s requestPermissions() output differs between activation and deactivation (due to code change, proxy pattern, or malicious behavior)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Kernel does not persist the granted permission set; it recomputes on deactivation
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During policy deactivation / after deactivation when stale permissions remain
